Rapport
A competency question
A hypothetical
I rebuilt the pricing model, which took margin from minus three to plus six per cent.
I cut the close from twelve days to five, which meant we reported a week earlier.
I renegotiated the covenant so that we kept eight per cent of headroom.
I disclosed the error early, resulting in zero audit adjustments.
Let's start with a competency question, Felipe. Tell me about a time you turned a struggling function around.

There was a division losing money every month. My job was to find out why, and the turning point was realising the pricing model was the root cause. I rebuilt it over one quarter, which took margin from minus three per cent to plus six.
Let me probe that. Plenty of people claim a turnaround. How do I know it was you, and not the market?

Fair question. Let me back it up with something tangible: the market was flat that quarter, and only the two products I repriced moved. I can quantify it: those two lines went from a nine per cent loss to breakeven, while the rest of the division was unchanged.
Good. Now a curveball: tell me about a real failure, and please don't give me I work too hard.

I won't dodge it. Early on, I under-hedged an FX exposure and it cost us four hundred thousand. It was my call and my mistake. What I did next is the part I'm proud of: I built the hedging policy that has caught every exposure since, zero losses in three years.
There was a problem; so I did one thing; and it took the number from here to there.
Let me back that up with something tangible I can quantify.
I won't dodge it. It was my call, it cost us this much, and here is what I changed.
If that happened, I'd quantify it, disclose it early, then fix the control.
That is exactly why this role, and this company, matter to me.

I place chief financial officers for a living, and I sit in on the final interviews, so I will tell you exactly what separates the two candidates who reach that room. It is never the CV. By the time you are in front of me, I already have your CV; reading it back to me is the fastest way to lose me. What wins is the story with a number at the end of it. The weaker candidate tells me what he was responsible for. He oversaw a team, he managed a budget, he was in charge of reporting. All true, and all useless, because it tells me nothing about what actually happened. The stronger candidate tells me the same career as a series of turning points. There was a problem, here is the one thing I did about it, and here is what it moved, from this number to that number, in this many months. Every claim is backed up by one tangible result. She does not say she is good under pressure; she tells me about the night she found the hole and what she did before breakfast. Give me the film, not the job description, and quantify the ending. That is the candidate I call back.

I coach senior executives before big interviews, and the question everyone dreads is the same one: tell me about a time you failed. People think it is a trap. It is the opposite. It is the most generous question in the interview, because it is the one place you get to prove you are honest, self-aware, and still standing. The mistake is to dodge it. You give a fake failure, a strength dressed up as a weakness, I work too hard, I care too much, and the interviewer stops trusting everything else you said. So here is the discipline. Name a real failure in one clean sentence. Do not flinch and do not over-explain. Then quantify what it cost, because a failure with a number is a failure you have actually understood. And then, most important, land the plane on what changed afterwards, the process you built, the mistake you never made twice. Own it, size it, and close on the lesson. Do that, and the hardest question in the room becomes the moment they decide to hire you.
